coredata.dc:title: Enhanced fluorescence of Zn-doped carbon quantum dots using zinc citrate chelate as precursor for fluorescent sensor applications
coredata.dc:creator: He, Huan
Yang, Yanan
Li, Junfeng
Lai, Xuefei
Chen, Xianfei
Wang, Li
Zhang, Wentao
Huang, Yi
Zhang, Peicong
coredata.dc:description: The Zn2+ doped Carbon Quantum Dots (Zn-CQDs) were synthesized with zinc citrate chelate as the precursor by a hydrothermal method. The evaluation results indicated that Zn2+ was successfully embedded in CQDs. Most of Zn2+ ions were combined with carboxyl groups on the surface of carbon dots to form Zn-O bonds and some Zn2+ ions were embedded in the interstice points of CQDs. The remaining Zn2+ were reduced to zinc atoms and adsorbed on the surface of CQDs. With the increase in Zn2+ concentration, the size of synthesized Zn-CQDs increased. When zinc ion concentration increased to 9%, the average size of Zn-CQDs grew up to 8 nm, but the size of undoped CQDs was just 1.5 nm. When Zn2+ concentration increased from 1% to 9%, the fluorescence intensity increased, while reaching above 9%, the fluorescence intensity showed a downward trend. When Zn2+ concentration was 7%, the fluorescence quantum yield of Zn-CQDs increased to 48%, but the fluorescence quantum yield of undoped CQDs was only 37%. With the increase in Zn2+ concentration, the PL spectrum of Zn-CQDs showed a red shift phenomenon. In addition, Zn-CQDs showed superior fluorescent sensing capability to Fe3+ and Hg2+, presenting greater fluorescence quenching effect on Fe3+ and Hg2+ than CA-CQDs. Therefore, Zn-CQDs demonstrated excellent fluorescence properties and can be applied in fluorescent sensor, biological imaging, optoelectronics, and catalysis fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zn-CQDs
Fluorescence intensity
Fluorescence quantum yield
Red shift
Fluorescent sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM images of CA-CQDs (a1-a2), Zn (3%)-CQDs (b1-b2) and Zn (9%)-CQDs (c1-c2).
Structure characterization of CA-CQDs and Zn (9%)-CQDs. A: XRD patterns of CA-CQDs and Zn (9%)-CQDs. B: Raman spectra of CA-CQDs and Zn (9%)-CQDs. C: EDS of Zn (9%)-CQDs.
XPS (A), C1s (B), O1s (C), Zn2p (D) and Zn LMM Auger spectra of Zn (9%)-CQDs.

Optical properties of CA-CQDs and Zn (1%)-CQDs. A. UV–Vis absorption of the CA-CQDs and Zn (1%)-CQDs. B. Emission spectra of CA-CQDs with excitation of different wavelength (300–380 nm). C. Emission spectra of Zn (1%)-CQDs with excitation of different wavelength (300–380 nm).

A. Fluorescence intensity comparison chart of Zn-CQDs with different concentrations of Zn2+ (0%, 1%, 3%, 5%, and 7%). B. Intensity curve of Zn-CQDs (x = 1%, 3%, 5%, and 7%) at λex = 340, λem = 426 nm.

A. Fluorescence intensity comparison chart of Zn-CQDs with different concentrations of Zn2+ (0%, 9%, 11%, 13%, and 15%). B Intensity curve of Zn-CQDs (x = 9%, 11%, 13%, and 15%) with excitation of 360 nm.
Simulated optimized structure of CA-CQDs, Zn1-CQDs and Zn2-CQDs.
Photographs of the solution of Zn-CQDs with different concentrations of Zn2+ (0%, 1%, 3%, 5%, 7%, 9%) under visible light (left) and under 365 nm UV light (right).

Influence of Fe3+ concentration on the fluorescence of CA-CQDs (A) and Zn(7%)-CQDs (C) and linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of CA-CQDs (B) and Zn(7%)-CQDs (D)and concentration of Fe3+.

Influence of Hg2+ concentration on the fluorescence of CA-CQDs (A) and Zn(7%)-CQDs (C) and linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of CA-CQDs (B) and Zn(7%)-CQDs (D)and concentration of Hg2+.

head.ce:title: Enhanced fluorescence of Zn-doped carbon quantum dots using zinc citrate chelate as precursor for fluorescent sensor applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials, Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu 610059, Sichuan, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials, Chemistry & Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials, Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu 610059, Sichuan, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, Sichuan, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, Sichuan, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu 610059, Sichuan, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environment, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu 610059, Sichuan, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The Zn2+ doped Carbon Quantum Dots (Zn-CQDs) were synthesized with zinc citrate chelate as the precursor by a hydrothermal method. The evaluation results indicated that Zn2+ was successfully embedded in CQDs. Most of Zn2+ ions were combined with carboxyl groups on the surface of carbon dots to form Zn-O bonds and some Zn2+ ions were embedded in the interstice points of CQDs. The remaining Zn2+ were reduced to zinc atoms and adsorbed on the surface of CQDs. With the increase in Zn2+ concentration, the size of synthesized Zn-CQDs increased. When zinc ion concentration increased to 9%, the average size of Zn-CQDs grew up to 8 nm, but the size of undoped CQDs was just 1.5 nm. When Zn2+ concentration increased from 1% to 9%, the fluorescence intensity increased, while reaching above 9%, the fluorescence intensity showed a downward trend. When Zn2+ concentration was 7%, the fluorescence quantum yield of Zn-CQDs increased to 48%, but the fluorescence quantum yield of undoped CQDs was only 37%. With the increase in Zn2+ concentration, the PL spectrum of Zn-CQDs showed a red shift phenomenon. In addition, Zn-CQDs showed superior fluorescent sensing capability to Fe3+ and Hg2+, presenting greater fluorescence quenching effect on Fe3+ and Hg2+ than CA-CQDs. Therefore, Zn-CQDs demonstrated excellent fluorescence properties and can be applied in fluorescent sensor, biological imaging, optoelectronics, and catalysis fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) have shown the fascinating application potential in bioimaging, optoelectronics, and catalysis due to their advantages, such as high chemical stability, high water dispersibility, easy functionalization, low toxicity, and high resistance to photobleaching [1,2], over semiconductor quantum dots and heavy-metal-based quantum dots [3,4]. However, existing CQDs present lower quantum yield and fluorescence intensity than traditional metal compound quantum dots, thus greatly limiting its application in LED display and biomedical fields [5–8]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop CQDs with higher quantum yield and high fluorescence intensity. Currently, emissive traps on the surface [9] and electronic conjugate structures [10] are the two strategies to interpret the PL mechanism of CQDs. In order to improve the quantum yield and fluorescence properties of CQDs, modification methods have been implemented. Among them, doping method is the main modification method of CQDs as it does not affect the inherent advantages of CQDs [11–13].

The traditional modification way of CQDs is to dope inorganic non-metallic elements [14–16] and covalent bonds (SC bond, PC bond and NC bond) can be easily formed based on the shared electronic pair between carbon and inorganic non-metal. The covalent bond plays a pivotal role in regulating the internal structure and enhancing the performance of CQDs. However, the fluorescence enhancement effect obtained after the introduction of non-metallic elements is still worse than the desired effect. Moreover, the synthesis efficiency or development degree of non-metallic doped CQDs is not better than that of undoped CQDs (CA-CQDs). Inorganic non-metallic doped CQDs still need further exploration.

Another modification way of CQDs is to dope metal ions. The predominant choice of doped metal ions are transition metals, such as zinc ions [17], gadolinium ions [18], and manganese ions [19]. However, zinc ions-doped CQDs aroused wide interest among researchers in recent years because of the easy availability, good solubility and negligible toxic character of zinc ions [17]. In 2014, Hailong Dong [5] mixed glycerol with zinc chloride and prepared zinc-doped CQDs by pyrolysis. Zinc-doped CQDs showed the improved fluorescence intensity compared with undoped CQDs, indicating that the dehydration and carbonization could be supported by adding zinc chloride. However, metal ions existed in an independent form in the carbon source solution and no chelation structure could be used to regulate the dispersion of metal ions in CQDs, thus resulting in the poor fluorescence enhancement effect. However, the existing form of zinc and the quantum yield and product yield of Zn-CQDs had not been explored. In order to control the dispersion of zinc ions and further improve the fluorescence performance of zinc ions doped CQDs, Poushali Das [17] introduced zinc chloride into the prepared CQDs solution and synthesized Zn-CQDs by a hydrothermal method, which showed advantageous fluorescent sensing capability in ion detection. Zinc ions successfully entered the CQDs and led to fluorescence spectrum red shift, but the introduction of zinc ions did not improve the luminescence intensity. The reason may be attributed to the way that zinc ions are introduced later.

Aiming at the shortcomings of the above two systems of zinc-doped CQDs, we proposed that CQDs with excellent fluorescence properties might be obtained if zinc ions existed in the form of bonds with a carbon source in the early stage. In this way, zinc ions could be evenly distributed in CQDs with chelate as the precursor during the carbonization process and then improve the fluorescence performance of CQDs to some degree. In addition, transition metals had a catalytic effect during the carbon material growth [20–24] and the development and synthesis efficiency of carbon dots may be promoted during the growth process in the presence of zinc ions. Therefore, the zinc ion doped CQDs with better fluorescence properties obtained with chelate as the precursor had been synthesized.

It was reported that citric acid and zinc ions were prone to form a chelate due to the pulling effect of the carboxyl group [25]. In this study, Zn2+ doped Carbon Quantum Dots (Zn-CQDs) were synthesized with zinc citrate chelate as precursor by a facile hydrothermal method. Our designed system had a series of advantages. Firstly, the excellent dispersion of zinc ions in carbon dots could be ensured with chelate as the precursor. Secondly, the synthesis efficiency of CQDs would be improved to some degree due to the carrying effect of chelate. Thirdly, Zn led to the growth of CQDs and the better and more completely structure could be developed under the catalysis of zinc. Finally, Zn-CQDs may trigger a phenomenon of red shift and improved fluorescent sensing capability. The fluorescence intensity and fluorescence quantum yield of Zn-CQDs may be higher than those of undoped CQDs.

Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O, 99.5%), zinc nitrate (Zn(NO3)2, 96.0%), anhydrous ethanol (C2H5OH, 99.7%), and acetone (CH3COCH3, AR), Ferric chloride (FeCl3, AR), mercury nitrate (Hg(NO3)2, AR), Potassium dichromate (K2(Cr2O7), AR), ammonium vanadate(NH4(VO3), AR) were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd., Chengdu, China. Distilled water was used in the experiment and all the chemicals were directly used without further purification.

Zn-CQDs were prepared via the hydrothermal method with zinc citrate chelate as the precursor. Firstly, 0.01 mol citric acid and a certain amount of zinc nitrate were mixed together and then dissolved in 30 mL of distilled water for 10-min constant stirring at room temperature. The molar mass of doped zinc nitrate was calculated based on 0.01 mol citric acid. Then, the as-obtained mixture solution was transferred to a 100.0-mL stainless steel reactor with teflon liner for the immediate hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained pale yellow product was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min to remove large particles and the supernatant was collected. Afterwards, the supernatant was transferred to a 10-mL dialysis kit (MWCO 500) and dialyzed in 1.5 L of distilled water for 48 h. In the dialysis process, distilled water was regularly replaced for eight times. Next, the Zn-CQDs solution was obtained after removing the fluid from the dialysis bag.

When a certain ratio of acetone was added to the suspensions, Zn-CQDs would precipitate. After about 10 min settlement, the turbid liquid was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min to obtain the Zn-CQDs precipitate, which could be dissolved in deionized water again. Lastly, a light greyish powder was obtained after drying the precipitate in a drying oven at 80 °C for 30 min. The product yield of Zn-CQDs powder was about 0.110 g per 2.1 g citric acid. Powder samples were used for the material analysis including XRD and EDS.

The crystal structures were analyzed by DX-2700 X-ray diffractometer (Dandong Fangyuan Instrument Co., Ltd.) with Cu Ka radiation (40 kV, 40 mA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope under an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Raman spectra were recorded on a Renishaw plus laser Raman spectrometer with λexc = 785 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) patterns were measured on a thermoelectron instrument (Thermo Scientific ESCALAB250) with Al Ka X-ray radiation as the excitation source (1486.8 eV, 500 mm). The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded with a Lambda 950 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The luminescence spectra were measured with Hitachi FL-4600 fluorescence spectrometer. All the measurements were performed at room temperature.

The quantum yields of Zn (7%)-CQDs and CA-CQDs were obtained with quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference. The quantum yield is calculated as:
(1)
Φ u
=
Φ s
Y u
/
Y s
A s
/
A u
n u
/
n s
,
where Φ is quantum yield; Y refers to measured integrated fluorescence emission intensity; A corresponds to absorbance measured at the excitation wavelength; n is the refractive index; the subscript “s” refers to the referenced fluorophore (quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4) with the known quantum yield; “u” refers to the unknown quantum yield of Zn-CQDs and CA-CQDs. In order to minimize the reabsorption, the absorption was kept below 0.05 at the excitation wavelength of 320 nm.

Fe3+ solutions with 10 various concentrations of 0–900 μmolL-1 were prepared. Successively, the 10 Fe3+ solutions were added respectively to CQDs or Zn-CQDs solution with concentration of 0.25 g/L at room temperature. Next, the fluorescence tests were performed after placed the mixed solutions for 12 h. In addition, the detection method of Hg2+ was in the same way as Fe3+ ions.

The CA-CQDs, Zn (3%)-CQDs and Zn (9%)-CQDs were characterized by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a1-a2, the as-synthesized CA-CQDs were black dots and the size ranged from 1.0 nm to 4.0 nm with an average diameter of 1.5 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image showed the lattice plane with an interfringe distance of 0.2756 nm, corresponding to the (010) plane of graphitic carbon. When the zinc ion concentration was 3% (Fig. 1b1-b2), a better and more completely structure had been developed under the catalysis effect of zinc and the size of the as-prepared Zn (3%)-CQDs became larger with an average diameter of 4 nm. The HRTEM image showed the lattice distance of 0.2481 nm, which also corresponded to the (010) planes of graphitic carbon. The increased size may be ascribed to the polymerized function of zinc ions. The molecular weight of Zn-CQDs increased with the increase in zinc ion concentration, thus resulting in the relatively large particle size. Furthermore, when zinc ion concentration increased to 9% (Fig. 1. c1- c2), the average diameter of Zn (9%)-CQDs particle size was slightly increased to 8 nm. The HRTEM of Zn (9%)-CQDs exhibited amorphous phase and no interfringe was observed. The changes might be interpreted as follows. The excessive introduction of zinc ions led to the polymerization of carbon dots and then resulted in the disordered crystal lattice.

In order to further analyze the structure and disorder degree of Zn-CQDs, The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns and Raman spectra of CA-CQDs and Zn (9%)-CQDs were obtained. Fig. 2 A shows the XRD patterns of CA-CQDs and Zn (9%)-CQDs. The as-prepared CA-CQDs belonged to the amorphous phase and there are two steamed bread peaks at 26° and 44°, which corresponded to the (002) and (101) planes of grapheme carbon, respectively. When zinc ion concentration increased to 9%, the XRD patterns of Zn (9%)-CQDs showed no significant difference compared to those of undoped CQDs. It could be concluded that a small amount of zinc ions would not influence the intrinsic structure of CQDs. Raman spectra of CA-CQDs and Zn (9%)-CQDs. The D-band at 1342.8 cm−1 was ascribed to sp3 carbon, whereas the G-band at 1634.1 cm−1 was ascribed to sp2 carbons (Fig. 2B). Compared with undoped CQDs, Zn (9%)-CQDs had the broadened D-band, which indicated that the defects and the degree of disorder of CQDs increased with the increase in Zn concentration. To further explore the elemental constitution of Zn (9%)-CQDs, the Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) in Fig. 2C was analyzed and the analysis results demonstrated that the elemental composition of Zn (9%)-CQDs included C (42.16%), O (41.81%), and Zn (16.02%).

The XPS spectrum (Fig. 3 A) showed three strong peaks at 1022.28 eV, 532.28 eV, and 284.28 eV, which were respectively ascribed to Zn2p, O1s, and C1s. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 3B) shows three peaks at 288.56 eV, 285.86 eV, and 284.72 eV, which were respectively assigned to the CO, COH, and CC groups. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 3C) had two peaks at 532.84 eV and 531.7 eV, which were respectively assigned to the COH/COC and CO/ZnO groups. Fig. 3D shows symmetrical peaks at 1045.26 eV and 1022.04 eV, which were respectively assigned to the Zn-2p1/2 and Zn-2p3/2 core levels of Zn-CQDs. The energetic separation of 23.1 eV was in good agreement with the related standard value (22.97 eV) [26], indicating that Zn species were in divalent (2+) valance state. Consequently, electronic or chemical changes in the Zn position were not found in Zn-2p foundation level XPS spectra. Furthermore, Auger peaks were concentrated and could better illustrate profile alterations than XPS spectrum for recognizing unusual oxidation states of species since three electrons were involved in numerous body effects in a distinct Auger transition [27].

To determine the electronic states of Zn species, Zn LMM Auger lines were used to calculate Zn interstitial against Zn-O. As shown in Fig. 3E, there are two peaks at the upper binding energy of 498.0 eV and the lower binding energy of 494.8 eV [27]. The comparatively normalized peak area calculated from the deconvoluted spectrum could be used to measure Zni defect concentration and Zn-O bond (Fig. 4 E). The Zn interstitial corresponded to a smaller area than the Zn-O bond, indicating that Zn-O bond was the dominant species.

Therefore, three different models were proposed for Zn-CQDs on the basis of different occupied sites of zinc ions. Partial zinc ions were combined with carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs and occupied carboxyl hydrogen. Some zinc ions were combined with two hydroxyl groups, occupied the hydrogen in the hollows of the CQDs and existed in the form of O-Zn-O. The remaining zinc ions were reduced to zinc atom and adsorbed on the surface of CQDs.

In order to display the introduction of Zn2+ on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs, the fluorescence properties of Zn-CQDs were further discussed. Fig. 4A presents the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the aqueous solutions of CA-CQDs and Zn (1%)-CQDs. Due to the π-π* transition of CC group, the as-prepared CA-CQDs exhibited a distinct characteristic peak at 270 nm and a shoulder peak at 310 nm, which were ascribed to the n-π* transition of the C0 group. When zinc ion concentration was 1%, the shoulder peak absorption band of Zn (1%)-CQDs was broadened from 250 nm to 450 nm. It could be concluded that the UV–Vis absorption intensity of CQDs increased after zinc ions were doped.

Under the light beam excitation of 300 to 380 nm, the as-prepared CA-CQDs and Zn (1%)-CQDs all showed excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission. The maximum emission of CA-CQDs was available at 414 nm under the maximum excitation at 320 nm (Fig. 4B). Under UV light irradiation at 365 nm, the CA-CQDs gave the dark blue light emission. The strongest emission of Zn (1%)-CQDs occurred at 426 nm under the excitation wavelength of 340 nm (Fig. 4C), thus giving a light blue fluorescence emission under the UV light irradiation at 365 nm. Compared to the fluorescence intensity of undoped CQDs, the fluorescence intensity of the Zn (1%)-CQDs was almost increased by four times from 119 to 550.

On the basis of high fluorescence intensity of Zn (1%)-CQDs, the effects of different Zn2+ concentrations (1%, 3%, 5%, 7%) on the fluorescent properties of the Zn-CQDs are further explored. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity of Zn-CQDs increased significantly with the increase in Zn2+ concentration. When the concentration increased to 7%, the fluorescence intensity reached 926, which was 8 times of that of CA-CQDs, 119. After the introduction of zinc ions, the redshift occurred compared to CA-CQDs. The optimal excitation wavelength shifted from 320 nm to 340 nm and the maximum PL position shifted from 414 nm (CA-CQDs) to 426 nm. The redshift distance was 12 nm.

On the basis of Zn (7%)-CQDs, the doping concentration of Zn2+ was further increased from 9% to 15%. Fig. 6 A shows the fluorescence intensity of Zn-CQDs under different concentrations of Zn2+ (0%, 9%, 11%, 13%, 15%). When the doping concentration was 9%, the strongest fluorescence intensity of the Zn (9%)-CQDs increased to 933, which was higher than Zn (7%)-CQDs. When Zn2+ doping concentration was increased above 9%, the fluorescence intensity of Zn-CQDs showed a decreasing trend. (Fig. 6B) The reason maybe that the high zinc ion concentration destroyed the structure of CQDs and led to fluorescence quenching. In addition, the strongest emission of Zn(9%, 11%, 13%, 15%)-CQDs were found at 442 nm under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Compared with CA-CQDs, the redshift occurred again at the maximum PL position from 414 nm (CA-CQDs) to 442 nm. The redshift distance was 28 nm.

From the analysis of fluorescence spectrum of Zn-CQDs, it could be concluded that a moderate amount of zinc ions could enhance the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs and promote the redshift of the fluorescence spectrum. Based on this conclusion, we speculated that the introduction of zinc ions could increase the fluorescence quantum yield or product yield of CQDs to some degree. Therefore, the product yields of Zn (7%)-CQDs and CA-CQDs were respectively obtained as 0.110 g and 0.020 g per 2.100 g citric acid. The weight of zinc ions could be neglected because of less amount of doped zinc ions. Furthermore, the fluorescence quantum yield of Zn (7%)-CQDs was 48%, whereas that of CA-CQDs was just 37%. The difference may be interpreted as follows. Citric acid was chelated with zinc ions to form a bundle structure in the precursor solution and the carbonization rate and size were improved compared to isolated citric acid due to the carrying and catalyze effects of Zn. In addition, in the growth process of carbon dots, the numbers of pores and interior disorder degree of CQDs gradually increased, thus improving the effective absorption of ultraviolet visible light and promoting the quantum yield to some degree.

To reveal the origin of observed red shift of fluorescence spectrum, the density-functional method as implemented in Dmol3 was adopted to investigate the energy-gap modulation of CQDs upon Zn doping. Fig. 7 shows the simulated atomic structures of CA-CQDs, Zn1-CQDs and Zn2-CQDs. Zn1-CQDs represent Zn2+ occupies the carboxyl hydrogen on the surface, Zn2-CQDs represent Zn2+ occupies the hydrogen in the hollows, as discussed in the above XPS part. Obviously, the crystal structures were all changed because Zn-CQDs unit cell would adjust itself to the most stable state [28]. The energy gaps between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of CA-CQDs, Zn1-CQDs and Zn2-CQDs clusters were respectively calculated to be 3.1048 eV, 1.0233 eV and 1.1257 eV. Obviously, the band gap decreased after the incorporation of zinc ions. According to the frontier orbit theory, redshift would emerge after the introduction of zinc ions because of the reduced band gap. The result was in line with experimental observations.

Additionally, due to the quantum confinement effect, the band gap of GQDs was highly influenced by their size when the diameter less than 10 nm. The TEM of Zn-CQDs indicated that the band gap of the GQDs would decrease with the increase in the size of GQDs after the introduction of Zn2+ ions, thus leading to the fluorescence spectroscopy red shift [29–31].

Fig. 8 shows the photograph of the Zn-CQDs with different concentrations of Zn2+ (0%, 1%, 3%, 5%, 7%, 9%) under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). The sample was changed from a white transparent solution into a pale yellow transparent solution with the increase in zinc ion concentration under sunlight lamp, indirectly proving that the introduction of zinc ions could promoted the formation of CQDs. Under the UV light irradiation at 365 nm, all samples exhibited blue emission from dark blue to white blue and fluorescence intensity increased with the increase in the Zn2+ concentration. The result was in accordance with the fluorescence spectrum of Zn-CQDs.

In order to verify the fluorescent sensing capability of carbon quantum dots in metal ions detection after zinc ion introduction, the prepared CA-CQDs and Zn-CQDs solutions were used to detect metal ions, as CQDs can be used as advanced fluorescent sensing platform for the detection of different analytes due to their intrinsic fluorescent properties. The principle for fluorescent sensors using CQDs is based on the fluorescence change caused by the introduction of analyte [32]. With Fe3+ concentration excessively higher or lower than normal, Fe3+ may cause various diseases, therefore, in this paper, the prepared CA-CQDs and Zn-CQDs solutions were used to detect Fe3+. The relevant fluorescence spectrum analysis was shown in Fig. 9 .

As presented Fig. 9A, the fluorescence intensity of CA-CQDs decreased when heightening Fe3+ concentration, indicating that Fe3+ had a certain quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of CA-CQDs. In addition, when Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0–500 μmolL-1 (Fig. 9B), a better linear fitting effect between the fluorescence intensity of the CA-CQDs and the concentration of iron ion was observed, i.e., R2 = 0.91694, the linear slope was 0.15065.

Fig. 9C showed the emission spectrum of Zn(7%)-CQDs in different concentrations of Fe3+. The fluorescence intensity of Zn(7%)-CQDs significantly reduced with increasing of Fe3+ concentration, indicating that it had a good sensitivity on Fe3+. Furtherly, the fluorescence intensity of Zn(7%)-CQDs correlated with Fe3+ concentration by linear fitting in the range of 0–500 μmolL-1, as shown in Fig. 9D (R2 = 0.90768, k = 1.17214).

From the above, Zn(7%)-CQDs as a fluorescent sensor demonstrated significant sensitivity to Fe3+, while the fluorescence quenching effect of Zn(7%)-CQDs on Fe3+ was stronger than that of CA-CQDs. The reason could be that the degree of disorder for Zn(7%)-CQDs increased after the introduction of zinc ions, which provided more binding sites for coordination with iron ions, resulting in a better quenching effect on Zn(7%)-CQDs [33].

A further detection of mercury ions was performed by the prepared CA-CQDs and Zn-CQDs solutions due to the fact that Hg2+ is a highly toxic heavy metal which is not biodegradable and can accumulated in people's lives and absorbed by the human body. The relevant fluorescence spectrum analysis was shown in Fig. 10 .

As presented in Fig. 10, the fluorescence intensity of CA-CQDs and Zn(7%)-CQDs decreased by increasing Hg2+ concentration, indicating that Hg2+ had a quenching effect on the fluorescence of CA-CQDs and Zn(7%)-CQDs. At the same time, the fluorescence intensity of CA-CQDs with Hg2+ concentration was observed by a linear correlation in the range of 0–500 μmolL-1. As shown in Fig. 10B, the slope was 0.08815, R2 = 0.95033. Similar correlation was found between fluorescence intensity of Zn(7%)-CQDs and Hg2+ concentration, with a higher k value (k = 0.85683, R2 = 0.77699), indicating that Zn(7%)-CQDs had a better sensitivity to Hg2+ ions, with a stronger fluorescence quenching effect than CA-CQDs. From these results it can be concluded that Zn(7%)-CQDs is an advantageous fluorescent sensor in Hg2+ detection.

In this study, Zn-CQDs were successfully prepared with zinc citrate chelate as the precursor. The synthesis efficiency of CQDs was improved due to the carrying effect of chelate. With the increase in zinc ion concentration, the size of Zn-CQDs increased. When zinc ions increased to 9%, the average size of Zn-CQDs grew up to 8 nm, but the size of CA-CQDs was just 1.5 nm. When zinc ion concentration increased from 1% to 9%, the fluorescence intensity increased. When Zn ion concentration increased above 9%, the fluorescence intensity showed a downward trend. The fluorescence quantum yield of Zn (7%)-CQDs increased to 48%, but that of CA-CQDs was just 37%. Furthermore, the redshift in the fluorescence spectrum of Zn-CQDs after the introduction of zinc ions had been confirmed by the energy band calculation and quantum size effect analysis. In addition, Zn-CQDs as a fluorescent sensor demonstrated significant improvement of sensitivity to Fe3+ and Hg2+, with a greater fluorescence quenching effect of Zn(7%)-CQDs on Fe3+ and Hg2+ than that of CA-CQDs. From the above, Zn-CQDs is a kind of CQDs with excellent fluorescence properties and can be used in fluorescent sensor, biological imaging, optoelectronics, and catalysis fields.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was jointly supported by the Science and Technology Innovation Miaozi Project of Sichuan Province (2018035), National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 41673109), the Science and Technology Department Project of Sichuan Province (2017SZ0185, 2018SZDZX0022).
